1: tefilah leChavaquq hanavi al shigionot: 'A prayer of Habakkuk the prophet, according to Shigionoth.' Chapter 3 is a psalm — the only prophet's book to contain one embedded as an integral unit. Shigionoth (H7692, plural of shiggayon) appears only here and Ps 7:1 — the term may indicate a passionate, wandering meter or a musical mode associated with intense lament/praise. The chapter is structured as a theophanic hymn: introduction (vv1-2), theophanic vision of YHWH the warrior (vv3-15), and personal response of trust (vv16-19). The psalm has a superscription and a postscript ('to the choirmaster, with stringed instruments,' v19) — indicating liturgical use in the temple.
2: YHWH shamati shimecha yareiti: 'LORD, I have heard the report of you, and your work, O LORD, do I fear.' The prophet's opening is a confession of awe at YHWH's past deeds (shamati shimecha, 'I heard your report/fame') — the reports of the Exodus and the conquest. Beyerev shanim chayehu — 'In the midst of the years revive it' — the prayer for YHWH to repeat his historic saving acts in the present crisis. Beqetzef rechem tizkor — 'in wrath remember mercy' — the tension at the heart of the book: divine wrath is real and the Babylonian judgment is coming, but Habakkuk prays that YHWH's foundational characteristic (rachem, compassion) will not be forgotten even in the midst of judgment.
3: Eloah miteman yavo veqadosh mehar Paran selah: 'God came from Teman, and the Holy One from Mount Paran. Selah.' The theophanic vision begins with the classic geography of the Sinai theophany: Teman (in Edom, south of Judah) and Mount Paran (in the Sinai/Negev region) are both associated with YHWH's original appearance to Israel at Sinai (Deut 33:2; Judg 5:4-5). The theophany is not a new divine arrival but a re-appearance of the same YHWH who came at Sinai. Kissa shamayim hodo — 'his glory covered the heavens' — the divine kavod (glory) is so vast it fills the sky. The selah marking (as in the Psalms) may indicate a musical interlude or a pause for reflection.
4: venogah kaor tihyeh qarnayim miyado lo: 'His brightness was like the light; rays flashed from his hand, and there he veiled his power.' The theophanic light-imagery: nogah (brightness/radiance, H5051) and or (light) combined — the blinding radiance of divine presence. Qarnayim miyado lo — 'rays from his hand' — the word qarnayim (H7161) means both 'horns' and 'rays of light' (the same ambiguity as Exod 34:29-35, where Moses's face 'shone' or had 'horns of light' after the theophany — hence Michelangelo's horned Moses). Vesham chevyon uzoh — 'there his power was hidden' — the paradox: the brilliance reveals and conceals simultaneously; the full power of YHWH is veiled even in theophany.
5: lefanav yelech daver veyetze reshef leraglav: 'Before him went pestilence, and plague followed at his heels.' The divine warrior is accompanied by his escort: dever (pestilence, H1698) and reshef (plague/burning disease, H7565) — also the names of Canaanite plague-deities demythologized in OT theology to become YHWH's servants. The cosmic battle imagery: YHWH's advance is preceded by forces that clear the way. In the Exodus narrative, the plagues were precisely this — YHWH's advance guard defeating Egypt before the main act of the sea crossing. The same theophanic warrior comes again in Hab 3.
6: amad vaymoded eretz raa vayyater goyim: 'He stood and measured the earth; he looked and shook the nations.' The divine warrior surveying the battlefield: amad vayamoded (stood and measured/shook) — the measuring (madad) may be the act of staking out territory for conquest, or the shaking (alternate reading of vayamoded from mud). Vayitpatzetzu harerei ad — 'then the eternal mountains were scattered' — the most permanent features of the landscape (the everlasting mountains) are shattered by YHWH's presence. The geological stability of mountains serves as the standard OT metaphor for permanence; their shattering before YHWH is the strongest statement of his sovereignty over creation.
7: tachat aven raiti ahalot Kushsan yirgezun yeri'ot eretz Midyan: 'I saw the tents of Cushan in affliction; the curtains of the land of Midian did tremble.' Cushan and Midian are south-eastern peoples associated with the Sinai wilderness period (Midian appears extensively in the Exodus narrative — Moses's father-in-law is Midianite). Their trembling before YHWH's advance rehearses the nations' fear at the original theophany (Exod 15:14-16: the peoples heard, they trembled; dread fell on the inhabitants of Canaan). The Hab 3 theophany re-enacts the Exodus theophany at cosmic scale.
8: havoraham YHWH bineharim af YHWH bineharim: 'Was your wrath against the rivers, O LORD? Was your anger against the rivers, or your indignation against the sea?' The repeated question about YHWH's action against rivers and sea draws on the creation combat myth (the divine warrior defeating the waters at creation, Ps 74:13-17; 89:9-10) and the Exodus sea-crossing (Exod 14-15). Ki tirkav al susecha — 'when you rode on your horses, on your chariot of salvation' — YHWH as the divine charioteer (cf. Ps 68:17: the chariots of God are tens of thousands). The 'chariot of salvation' (merkevotecha yeshuah) links the theophanic vision directly to the book's central concern: divine salvation for the people.
9: erya teria qashetecha shevuot mattot omer selah: 'You stripped the sheath from your bow, calling for many arrows. Selah.' The divine warrior prepares for battle: the bow unsheathed, the arrows readied. Shevuot mattot omer — 'the oaths of the tribes, yes, the word' — YHWH's battle preparation is grounded in covenant oath to the tribes of Israel. Neharot tebaqqa eretz — 'you split the earth with rivers' — the cosmic battle creates rivers where there were none (cf. Exod 17:6, water from the rock; Ps 78:15-16).
10: rauchah vayachilu harim zorem mayim avar natan tehom qolo: 'The mountains saw you and writhed; the raging waters swept on; the deep gave forth its voice; it lifted its hands on high.' The personification of the cosmic forces: mountains writhe (yachilu, the verb for labor pains — the same mountains that 'tremble like a woman in labor' in Isa 13:8), waters surge, the tehom (the deep/abyss, H8415 — the primordial ocean of Gen 1:2) lifts its hands in surrender. The posture of submission from creation itself before the divine warrior.
11: shemesh yerach amad zevulah leor chitzeicha yehalekhu: 'The sun and moon stood still in their place at the light of your arrows as they sped, at the flash of your glittering spear.' The theophany evokes the Joshua 10:12-13 solar standstill at Gibeon — the day the sun and moon stopped to allow Israel's victory. YHWH's theophanic arrows (chitzeicha) produce a flash of light that overshadows even the sun. The cosmic standstill before divine power: the celestial timekeepers are suspended at YHWH's battle initiative.
12: bezaem titzad eretz beaf tedush goyim: 'In fury you marched through the earth; in anger you trampled the nations.' The divine warrior in full battle advance: titzad (marched/strode) with zaem (fury/indignation, H2195) and tedush (trampled, from dush = to thresh, H1758). The threshing/trampling metaphor for divine judgment is common in the prophets (Isa 41:15; Mic 4:13; Amos 1:3). Nations are the grain threshed by the divine warrior in his anger.
13: yatzata leyeshua amecha leyeshua et meshichecha: 'You went out for the salvation of your people, for the salvation of your anointed.' The purpose of the theophanic battle is now declared: yeshua amecha (salvation of your people) and yeshua et meshichecha (salvation of your anointed). Meshichecha (your anointed, H4899) — the same root as mashiach (Messiah/Christ). In the immediate context this refers to the Davidic king (YHWH's anointed); in the canonical context, it points forward to the ultimate Anointed One. Machatzta rosh mibbeit rasha — 'you crushed the head of the house of the wicked' — the head-crushing echoes Gen 3:15.
14: nakapta bematav rosh peruzav: 'You pierced with his own arrows the heads of his warriors.' The reversal: the enemy's own weapons are turned against him. Peruzav (his warriors/those who rushed against me) — YHWH uses the enemy's own military strength against itself. The verse describes YHWH's battle-reversal tactic that appears throughout Israel's warfare theology: Israel does not fight; YHWH defeats the enemy through his own devices. The final phrase: kesofam le'echol ani bestrom — 'they came like a whirlwind to scatter me, rejoicing as if to devour the poor in secret.'
15: darcheta vayam susecha chomer mayim rabbim: 'You trampled the sea with your horses, the surging of mighty waters.' The theophany concludes where it began — with the divine warrior on his chariot-horses treading through the sea. The Exodus crossing is the culmination of the vision: YHWH's battle chariot treads through the sea, the great chaos-waters, affirming his sovereign power over all that threatens his people. The theophany (vv3-15) moves from Sinai to the present Babylonian threat to the eschatological victory — one continuous divine battle for the salvation of his anointed.
16: shamati vatirgazez bitni laqol tzalelu sefatai: 'I heard, and my body trembled; my lips quivered at the sound.' The transition from the theophanic vision (vv3-15) to personal response (vv16-19): the physical effect of the theophany on the prophet. Vatirgazez bitni (my body trembled, literally 'my belly quivered') — the visceral response to divine presence, like Daniel (Dan 8:27) and John (Rev 1:17) after visions. Ve'acharish asher anuach leyom tzarah — 'I quietly wait for the day of trouble to come upon people who invade us' — Habakkuk resolves to be still and wait (anuach, to rest) in the midst of impending judgment. The key: leishuah el uf (ascend to the people who will invade us) — awaiting YHWH's final victory.
17: ki te'enah lo tifreach vein yevul bagefanim kichesh maase zayit: 'Though the fig tree should not blossom, nor fruit be on the vines, the produce of the olive fail and the fields yield no food.' The hypothetical of total agricultural collapse: the fig tree (te'enah), vines (gefen), olives (zayit), and fields (shedamot) are the four pillars of the Levantine agricultural economy, representing the covenantal abundance promised in Deuteronomy (Deut 8:7-8). Their total failure would mean the complete withdrawal of covenant blessing — the covenant curses of Deut 28 realized. Habakkuk's resolution to rejoice despite this ultimate negative scenario is the book's climactic statement of faith.
18: va'ani baYHWH a'alozah agila be'elohei yishi: 'Yet I will rejoice in the LORD; I will take joy in the God of my salvation.' The emphatic va'ani (but I/yet I) sets Habakkuk apart from circumstances: regardless of the total agricultural collapse described in v17, his joy is grounded in YHWH and in the 'God of my salvation' (elohei yishi). The verb a'alozah (I will exult/rejoice, H5937) and agila (I will rejoice, H1523) are both strong verbs of celebration — not resigned contentment but active, exultant joy. The theological achievement of the book: the prophet moves from complaint about divine injustice (ch1) to joyful trust in the God of salvation (ch3:18) through the anchoring vision of divine sovereignty.
19: YHWH Adonai cheli vayasem raglai kaayalot: 'God, the Lord, is my strength; he makes my feet like the deer's, and makes me tread on my high places.' The closing verse of the book combines military and pastoral imagery: cheli (my strength/army, H2428 — the same word as chayil, valor/force) — YHWH as the prophet's military strength. Raglai kaayalot (my feet like deer's feet) — the sure-footedness of the mountain deer on dangerous terrain; Ps 18:33 uses the same image for David after battle victories. Vedarech bamotai (he makes me tread on my high places/heights) — the prophet-as-watchman who climbed to his tower in 2:1 now treads confidently on the heights with divine strength. The postscript (lamnatzeach bineginotai, to the choirmaster, with my stringed instruments) frames the entire ch3 as a liturgical psalm — the theodicy of faith finds its natural home in worship.
